steps.text: Find the y-intercept of function I: $f(x) = a(2.1)^x + b$. Set $x=0$: $f(0) = a(2.1)^0 + b = a(1) + b = a + b$. The y-intercept is $a+b$.
Find the y-intercept of function II: $g(x) = a(2.1)^{x+b}$ (the exponent includes $b$). Set $x=0$: $g(0) = a(2.1)^{0+b} = a \cdot (2.1)^b$. The y-intercept is $a \cdot (2.1)^b$.

Check whether each equation displays its y-intercept as a standalone constant or coefficient. In I, the y-intercept $a+b$ is a sum of two parameters — neither $a$ alone nor $b$ alone equals the y-intercept. In II, the y-intercept $a \cdot (2.1)^b$ is not visible as any coefficient or constant term in $a(2.1)^{x+b}$. Neither equation displays its y-intercept directly.

Evaluate each choice:
  • A: I only — I does not display $a+b$ as a single constant. Eliminated.
  • B: II only — II does not display $a(2.1)^b$ as a constant. Eliminated.
  • C: I and II — neither displays its y-intercept directly. Eliminated.
  • D: Neither I nor II — confirmed: neither equation has the y-intercept value visible as a standalone constant or coefficient. That matches choice D — D is correct.
